1.description: Funding conditions proxies. Panel A:TED$TED$spread measure. Panel B:FG$FG$funding conditions measure from Fontaine and Garcia (2012). Panel C:HPW$HPW$noise measure from Hu, Pan, and Wang (2013). Monthly data, 1986 to 2021. Vertical lines denote: (A) 1987 stock market crash, (B) 1994 bond market massacre, (C) LTCM bailout, (D) turn of the millennium, (E) Ford & GM downgrades, (F) 2008 financial crisis, (G) first Greece bailout, (H) second Greece bailout, (I) oil & China sell-off, and (J) COVID-19 financial crisis. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Impulse response functions. This figure plots the impulse responses ofLEVt+h$LEV_{t+h}$andFUNDt+h$FUND_{t+h}$to identified leverage supply shocksets$e_t^s$and demand shocksetd$e_t^d$over horizons ofh=1…20$h=1\ldots 20$quarters. The responses, plotted on they$y$-axis, are accompanied by 95% bootstrap confidence intervals in dashed blue lines. The responses are scaled in units of standard deviations of each variable of interest. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Historical decomposition. This figure decomposes the leverage forecast errors in terms of leverage demand and supply shocks from the econometric model identified with sign and asset pricing restrictions. See SectionIfor a description of the model and SectionIIfor details of the estimation. Quarterly data, 1986Q2 to 2021Q4. Vertical lines denote: (A) 1987 stock market crash, (B) 1994 bond market massacre, (C) LTCM bailout, (D) turn of the millenium, (E) Ford & GM downgrades, (F) 2008 financial crisis, (G) first Greece bailout, (H) second Greece bailout, and (I) oil & China sell-off. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Price of leverage demand and supply risks. Panel A: bootstrap distribution, point estimate and confidence interval of the price of riskλ$\lambda$of leverage demand and supply shocks from the econometric model identified with sign and asset pricing restrictions. Panel B: bootstrap distribution, sample value, and confidence interval for the uncenteredR2¯$\bar{R^2}$. See SectionIfor a description of the model and SectionII.Efor details of the estimation. See SectionIIIof theInternet Appendixfor the bootstrap procedure. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Leverage demand and supply shocks in asset pricing. This figure presents realized and fitted mean excess returns for test around assets and a 45∘$^\circ$line to assess the fit, including3×10$3 \times 10$and3×10$3 \times 10$equity and corporate bond portfolios sorted byβΔilliq$\beta ^{\Delta \text{illiq}}$,βΔσ$\beta ^{\Delta \sigma }$, andβΔFUND$\beta ^{\Delta \text{FUND}}$, labeled asLIQ$LIQ$,VOL$VOL$,FND$FND$(for equities) andCBL$CBL$,CBV$CBV$,CBF$CBF$(for corporate bonds), ordered from 1 to 10,2×6$2 \times 6$portfolios of recently issued and old Treasury bonds with different maturities, labeled asBND$BND$and ordered from 1 to 12, and2×27$2 \times 27$unlevered call and put options, labeled asCLL$CLL$andPUT$PUT$. Panel A: results using leverage demand and leverage supply shocks. Panel B: results using market returns and the AEM leverage factor. Each model is estimated using two-stage regressions without an intercept. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Set of identified parameters. This figure plots the set of parameters satisfying the identification restriction but not imposing symmetry. Panel A: prices of riskλd$\lambda _d$andλs$\lambda _s$. Panel B: the dispersions parametersωd$\omega _d$andωs$\omega _s$in the cross-section ofβi,d$\beta _{i,d}$andβi,s$\beta _{i,s}$. Panel C:LEV$LEV$loadingsbd$b_d$andbs$b_s$. Panel D:FUND$FUND$loadingsad$a_d$andas$a_s$. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: First-stageβ$\beta$estimates. This figure shows first-stageβ$\beta$estimates of leverage supply and demand shocks from single-factor models, with leverage demand shocks reported on they$y$-axis and leverage supply shocks on thex$x$-axis. Panel A: equity portfolios labeledLIQ$LIQ$,VOL$VOL$andFND$FND$. Panel B: Treasury bonds and corporate bond portfolios labeledCBL$CBL$,CBV$CBV$,CBF$CBF$, andBND$BND$. Panel C: unlevered options portfolios labeledPUT$PUT$andCLL$CLL$.

8.description: Price of leverage risk. This figure provides a scatter plot of raw leverage riskλj,l$\lambda _{j,l}$, across various test assets. Thex-axis showsλ̂j,lols$\hat{\lambda }^{ols}_{j,l}$estimates from the second-stage OLS regressions, whiley-axis displaysλ̂j,l$\hat{\lambda }_{j,l}$estimates from the econometric model in SectionII. The dashed line represents the 45∘$^\circ$line, which would correspond to a perfect fit. We also report theR2$R^2$for the exact-fit regressionλ̂j,lols=λ̂j,l+ξj$\hat{\lambda }^{ols}_{j,l} = \hat{\lambda }_{j,l} + \xi _j$. The asset groups are as follows: PUT and CALL represent unlevered S&P 500 option portfolios for puts and calls, respectively. VOL, LIQ, and FND are three sets of 10 equity portfolios sorted by volatility, illiquidity, and funding risk, respectively. CBV, CBL, and CBF are three sets of 10 corporate bond portfolios sorted by the same criteria. BND represents a set of Treasury bonds. The ”Stocks” category combines VOL, LIQ, and FND portfolios, “Bonds” includes CBV, CBL, CBF, and BND, while “Options” consists of PUT and CALL portfolios. For additional details, refer to SectionIVof theInternet Appendix. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
